content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/1909.02244
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/1909.02244/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/1909.02244

Begin by reading paper.md.

1	## Robust Navigation with Language Pretraining and Stochastic Sampling
     2	
     3	Xiujun Li ♠♦ Chunyuan Li ♦ Qiaolin Xia ♣ Yonatan Bisk ♠♦♥
     4	
     5	Asli Celikyilmaz ♦ Jianfeng Gao ♦ Noah A. Smith ♠♥ Yejin Choi ♠♥
     6	
     7	♠ Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering, University of Washington ♣ Peking University ♦ Microsoft Research AI ♥ Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence { }
     8	
     9	xiujun,ybisk,nasmith,yejin @cs.washington.edu xql@pku.edu.cn { xiul,chunyl,jfgao } @microsoft.com
    10	
    11	## Abstract
    12	
    13	Core to the vision-and-language navigation (VLN) challenge is building robust instruction representations and action decoding schemes, which can generalize well to previously unseen instructions and environments. In this paper, we report two simple but highly effective methods to address these challenges and lead to a new state-of-the-art performance. First, we adapt large-scale pretrained language models to learn text representations that generalize better to previously unseen instructions. Second, we propose a stochastic sampling scheme to reduce the considerable gap between the expert actions in training and sampled actions in test, so that the agent can learn to correct its own mistakes during long sequential action decoding. Combining the two techniques, we achieve a new state of the art on the Room-toRoom benchmark with 6% absolute gain over the previous best result (47% → 53%) on the Success Rate weighted by Path Length metric.
    14	
    15	## 1 Introduction
    16	
    17	The vision-and-language navigation (VLN) task, learning to navigate in visual environments based on natural language instructions, has attracted interest throughout the artificial intelligence research community (Hemachandra et al., 2015; Anderson et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Savva et al., 2019). It fosters research on multimodal representations and reinforcement learning, and serves as a test bed for many real-world applications such as in-home robots.
    18	
    19	In the recent Room-to-Room (R2R) VLN challenge (Anderson et al., 2018), most state-of-theart methods are developed based on an encoderdecoder framework (Cho et al., 2014; Sutskever et al., 2014), where a natural language instruction is represented as a sequence of words, and a navigation trajectory as a sequence of actions, enhanced with attention (Anderson et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Fried et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019a). Two important components are shared by all VLN agents: (i) an Instruction Encoder that employs a language model (LM) for instruction understanding; and (ii) an Action Decoder , where an appropriate sequence-level training scheme is required for sequential decision-making. Each component faces its own challenges (see Figure 1).
    20	
    21	Figure 1: Two challenges in VLN.
    22	
    23	<!-- image -->
    24	
    25	The first challenge is generalizing grounded natural language instruction understanding from seen to unseen environments. Specifically, in the R2R task, only 69% of bigrams are shared between training and evaluation. 1 Existing work leverages pretrained GloVe embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) to help generalize. In computer vision, it has been shown that large-scale models pretrained on ImageNet can transfer the knowledge to downstream applications (Yosinski et al., 2014), thus improving generalization. Comparable language-based transfer learning has not been shown for instruction understanding in VLN.
    26	
    27	The second challenge is exposure bias (Ranzato et al., 2016) for the action decoder, due to the discrepancy between training and inference. This problem is common to many tasks where decoding is needed, including text generation, abstractive summarization, and machine translation (Ben- gio et al., 2015). Two widely used training strategies are student-forcing and teacher-forcing (described in detail in Section 2.2). It is well-known that the sequence length determines which training strategy is more effective. In the VLN literature, student-forcing has been widely used, as early work (Anderson et al., 2018) used long trajectories (up to 20 steps) with a simple discrete action space. Most recent work, however, has relied on a panoramic action space (Fried et al., 2018) in which most trajectories are only up to seven steps long. In such cases, teacher-forcing is preferable (Tan et al., 2019). Neither strategy is perfect: teacher-forcing has exposure bias, while studentforcing's random actions can cause an agent to deviate far from the correct path, rendering the original instruction invalid. 2
    28	
    29	1 Table 1 shows n -gram overlap statistics between training seen and validation seen/unseen environments.
    30	
    31	Table 1: N-grams instruction overlap statistics between validation seen and unseen environments.
    32	
    33	| n-gram(s)   | Validation Seen   | Validation Unseen   |
    34	|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
    35	| 1 2 3 4     | 87.2%             | 80.7%               |
    36	|             | 77.4%             | 68.9%               |
    37	|             | 65.6%             | 57.3%               |
    38	|             | 50.8%             | 44.4%               |
    39	
    40	To tackle these challenges, we have developed two techniques to enable the agent to navigate more efficiently. For the first challenge, we leverage the recent large-scale pretrained language models, BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and GPT (Radford et al., 2018), to improve the agent's robustness in unseen environments. We show that large-scale language-only pretraining improves generalization in grounded environments. For the second challenge, we propose a stochastic sampling scheme to balance teacher-forcing and student-forcing during training, so that the agent can recover from its own mistakes at inference time. As a result of combining both techniques, on the R2R benchmark test set, our agent (PRESS) 3 achieves 53% on SPL, an absolute 6% gain over the current state of the art.
    41	
    42	## 2 Method
    43	
    44	In the VLN task, instructions are represented as a set X = { x i } i M =1 of M instructions per trajectory.
    45	
    46	2 To compensate, beam search is often used to improve success rates. Recent work, e.g., using search strategies (Ke et al., 2019) or progress monitors (Ma et al., 2019b), has focused on mitigating the cost of computing topk rollouts.
    47	
    48	3 PRE TRAINED LMS AND S TOCHASTIC S AMPLING
    49	
    50	Figure 2: Illustration of proposed methods.
    51	
    52	<!-- image -->
    53	
    54	Each instruction x i is a sequence of L i words, x i = [ x i, 1 , x i, 2 , ..., x i,L i ] . Given X , the goal is to train an agent to navigate from a starting position s 0 to a target position, via completing a T -step trajectory τ = [ s 0 , a 0 , s 1 , a 1 , · · · , s T , a T ] , where s t and a t are the visual state and navigation action, respectively, at step t . The training dataset D E = { τ , X} consists of example pairs of instruction set X and a corresponding expert trajectory τ . Our goal is to learn a policy π θ ( τ |X ) that maximizes the log-likelihood of the target trajectory τ given instructions X :
    55	
    56	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    57	
    58	where θ are trainable parameters. The policy is usually parameterized as an attention-based seq2seq model, with a language encoder z t = f θ E ( x ) , and an action decoder a t = f θ D ( z t , s t ) . Successful navigation depends on ( i ) precisely grounding the instructions X in τ in various environments, and ( ii ) correctly making the current decision a t based on previous actions/observations τ <t = [ s 0 , a 0 , · · · , s t -1 ] . To address these concerns, we propose PRESS, illustrated in Figure 2.
    59	
    60	## 2.1 Instruction Understanding with Pretrained Language Models
    61	
    62	At each step t , the agent decides where to navigate by updating a dynamic understanding of the instructions z t , according to its current visual state s t . Given instruction x , the language encoder proceeds in two steps, end-to-end, by considering a function decomposition f θ E = f θ x → e ◦ f θ e → z :
    63	
    64	- f θ x → e : x → e , where x = [ x 1 , · · · , x L ] is represented as its (contextualized) word embedding form e = [ e 1 , · · · , e L ] , with e i as the representation for word x i ;
    65	- f θ e → z : e → z t : For each embedded instruction e , we ground its representations as c i,t for state s t via neural attention. To handle
    66	
    67	language variability, one may aggregate features of multiple instructions C t = { c i,t } i M =1 into a single joint feature z t = 1 M ∑ M i =1 c i,t . 4
    68	
    69	Previous methods in VLN learn e either from pretrained word embeddings (Pennington et al., 2014) which do not take into account word context, or from scratch. As a result, their representations do not capture contextual information within each instruction. More importantly, they tend to overfit the training instructions associated with seen environments, limiting their utility in unseen environments. To remedy these issues, we propose to represent e with contextualized word embeddings produced using large-scale pretrained language models, such as BERT and GPT.
    70	
    71	Instruction Encoder. The agent's memory vector h t -1 captures the perception and action history and is used to attend to the instruction x . A pretrained LM f θ x → e encodes the instruction e = [ e 1 , · · · , e L ] ; e i where the representation for word x i , is built with f θ x → e ∈ { GPT, BERT } , and θ x → e are fine-tuned parameters. The embedded words e = [ e 1 , · · · , e L ] are passed through an LSTM f θ e → z to produce a sequence of textual features [ h e 1 , · · · , h e L ] . At each time step t , the textual context for the instruction x is computed as weighted sum of textual features in the sequence:
    72	
    73	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    74	
    75	where α l = Softmax ( h glyph[latticetop] t h e l ) , α l places more weight on the word representations that are most relevant to the agent's current status.
    76	
    77	Decoder. At each step, the agent takes an action a t , and the environment returns new visual observations; the agent first performs one-hop visual attention f ( · ) to all the visual image features s t , based on its previous memory vector h t -1 . Then, the agent updates its visual state s t as the weighted sum of the panoramic features, s t = ∑ j γ t,j s t,j . The attention weight γ t,j for the j -th visual feature s t,j represents its importance with respect to the previous history context h t -1 , computed as γ t,j = Softmax (( W h h t -1 ) glyph[latticetop] W s s t,j ) (Fried et al., 2018) where Softmax ( r j ) = exp( r j ) / ∑ j ′ exp( r j ′ ) , W h and W s are trainable projection matrices.
    78	
    79	4 This recovers z t = c t when only a single instruction is available.
    80	
    81	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    82	
    83	where a t -1 is the action taken at previous step, and θ D are the LSTM decoder parameters.
    84	
    85	Two-stage learning. The parameters of our agent are θ = { θ x → e , θ e → z , θ D } . In practice, we find that the agent overfits quickly, when the full model is naively fine-tuned, with θ x → e initialized by pretrained LMs ( e.g., BERT). In this paper, we consider a two-stage learning scheme to facilitate the use of pretrained LMs for VLN. ( i ) Embedding-based stage: We fix θ x → e , and use BERT or GPT to provide instruction embeddings. Only { θ e → z , θ D } are updated (while tuning on validation). ( ii ) Fine-tuning stage: We train all model parameters θ with a smaller learning rate, so that θ x → e can adapt to our VLN task.
    86	
    87	## 2.2 Stochastic Action Sampling
    88	
    89	A core question is how to learn useful state representations s t in Eq. (1) during the trajectory rollout. In other words, which action should we use to interact with the environment to elicit the next state? As noted, most existing work uses one of two schemes: ( i ) Teacher-forcing (TF) , where the agent takes ground-truth actions a T only. Though TF enables efficient training, it results in 'exposure bias' because agents must follow learned rather than gold trajectories at test time. In contrast, ( ii ) Student-forcing (SF) , where an action a S is drawn from the current learned policy, allows the agent to learn from its own actions (aligning training and evaluation), however, it is inefficient, as the agent explores randomly when confused or in the early stages of training.
    90	
    91	In this work, we consider a stochastic scheme ( SS ) to alternate between choosing actions from a T and a S for state transition s ← g ( a T , a S ) , inspired by scheduled sampling (Bengio et al., 2015). As illustrated in Figure 2, at each step, the agent 'flips a coin' with some probability glyph[epsilon1] to decide whether to take the teacher's action a T or a sampled one a S :
    92	
    93	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    94	
    95	where δ ∼ Bernoulli ( glyph[epsilon1] ) . This allows the agent to leverage the advantages of both TF and SF, yielding a faster and less biased learner. We fix glyph[epsilon1] as a constant during learning, which is different from the decaying schedule in (Bengio et al., 2015).
    96	
    97	## 3 Experiments
    98	
    99	## 3.1 Dataset
   100	
   101	We use the Room-to-Room dataset for the VLN task, built upon the Matterport3D dataset (Chang et al., 2017), which consists of 10,800 panoramic views and 7,189 trajectories. Each trajectory is paired with three natural language instructions. The R2R dataset consists of four splits: train seen, validation seen, validation unseen, and test unseen. There is no overlap between seen and unseen environments. At the beginning of each episode, the agent starts at a specific location, and is given natural instructions, the goal of the agent is to navigate to the target location as quickly as possible.
   102	
   103	## 3.2 Baseline Systems
   104	
   105	Wecompare our approach with eight recently published systems:
   106	
   107	- RANDOM: an agent that randomly selects a direction and moves five step in that direction (Anderson et al., 2018).
   108	- SEQ2SEQ: sequence-to-sequence model proposed by Anderson et al. as a baseline for the R2R benchmark (Anderson et al., 2018) and analyzed in (Thomason et al., 2019).
   109	- RPA (Wang et al., 2018): is an agent which combines model-free and model-based reinforcement learning, using a look-ahead module for planning.
   110	- SPEAKER-FOLLOWER (Fried et al., 2018): an agent trained with data augmentation from a speaker model with panoramic actions.
   111	- SMNA (Ma et al., 2019a): an agent trained with a visual-textual co-grounding module and progress monitor on panoramic actions.
   112	- RCM+SIL(TRAIN) (Wang et al., 2019): an agent trained with cross-modal grounding locally and globally via reinforcement learning.
   113	- REGRETFUL (Ma et al., 2019b): an agent with a trained progress monitor heuristic for search that enables backtracking.
   114	- FAST (Ke et al., 2019): an agent which combines global and local knowledge to compare partial trajectories of different lengths, enabling efficient backtrack after a mistake.
   115	- ENVDROP (Tan et al., 2019): proposed an environment dropout method, which can generate more environments based on the limited seen environments.
   116	
   117	Table 2: Comparison of PRESS and seq2seq.
   118	
   119	| Setting   |               | Validation Seen   | Validation Seen   | Validation Unseen   | Validation Unseen   |
   120	|-----------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
   121	|           | Agent         | SR ↑              | SPL ↑             | SR ↑                | SPL ↑               |
   122	| S         | seq2seq PRESS | 51 47 ( -4 )      | 46 43 ( -3 )      | 32 43 ( +11 )       | 25 38 ( +13 )       |
   123	| M         | seq2seq PRESS | 49 56 ( +7 )      | 44 53 ( +9 )      | 33 56 ( +23 )       | 26 50 ( +24 )       |
   124	
   125	## 3.3 Evaluation Metrics
   126	
   127	Webenchmark our agent on the following metrics:
   128	
   129	- TL Trajectory Length measures the average length of the navigation trajectory.
   130	- NE Navigation Error is the mean of the shortest path distance in meters between the agent's final location and the target location.
   131	- SR Success Rate with which the agent's final location is less than 3 meters from the target.
   132	- SPL Success weighted by Path Length trades-off SR against TL .
   133	
   134	SPL is the recommended primary metric, other metrics are considered as auxiliary measures.
   135	
   136	## 3.4 Implementation
   137	
   138	We use a LSTM/GPT/BERT for the language encoder, and a second single-layer LSTM for the action decoder (h=1024). We use Adamax and batch sizes of 24/16 for pretraining/finetuning. The learning rates for MLE are 1 e -4 , during finetuning BERT the learning rate is 5 e -5 . Following (Fried et al., 2018), we use a panoramic action space and the ResNet image features provided by (Anderson et al., 2018). The code is publicly available here: https://github.com/xjli/r2r\_vln .
   139	
   140	## 3.5 Results
   141	
   142	Robust Generalization. First, we compare PRESS to a baseline seq2seq model 5 in two evaluation settings on the validation splits: (1) S : A single instruction is provided to the agent at a time. Thus, three separate navigation trajectories are generated corresponding to three alternative instructions in this setting. We report the averaged performance over three separate runs. (2) M : All three instructions are provided to the agent at once. The seq2seq baseline does not have an aggregation strategy so we report its performance for the single trajectory with maximum likelihood. For PRESS, we aggregate the instructions via context mean-pooling and generate a single trajectory. No data augmentation is applied to either model.
   143	
   144	5 The baseline seq2seq agent is the FOLLOWER of SPEAKER-FOLLOWER (Fried et al., 2018).
   145	
   146	Table 3: Comparison with the state-of-the-art methods. Blue indicates best value overall.
   147	
   148	|                  |       | Validation Seen   | Validation Seen   | Validation Seen   | Validation Unseen   | Validation Unseen   | Validation Unseen   | Validation Unseen   | Test Unseen   | Test Unseen   | Test Unseen   | Test Unseen   |

   157	| REGRETFUL        | -     | 3.23              | 69                | 63                | -                   | 5.32                | 50                  | 41                  | 13.69         | 5.69          | 48            | 40            |
   158	| FAST             | -     | -                 | -                 | -                 | 21.17               | 4.97                | 56                  | 43                  | 22.08         | 5.14          | 54            | 41            |

   162	
   163	The results are summarized in Table 2. ( i ) PRESS drastically outperforms the seq2seq models on unseen environments in both settings, and ( ii ) Interestingly, our method shows a much smaller gap between seen and unseen environments than seq2seq. It demonstrates the importance of pretrained LMs and stochastic sampling for strong generalization in unseen environments.
   164	
   165	Comparison with SoTA. In Table 3, we compare the performance of our agent against all the published methods, our PRESS agent outperforms the existing models on nearly all the metrics.
   166	
   167	Ablation Analysis. Key to this work is leveraging large-scale pretrained LMs and effective training strategies for action sequence decoding. Table 4 shows an ablation of these choices. (1) BERT and GPT are better than LSTM on both seen and unseen environments, and BERT generalizes better than GPT on unseen environments. (2) Teacher-forcing performs better than studentforcing on validation unseen environments, while an opposite conclusion is drawn on validation seen environments. SS performs the best on unseen environments.
   168	
   169	Qualitative Examples. We provide two navigation examples of PRESS on the validation unseen environments with the step-by-step views and topdown views in Appendix.
   170	
   171	(1) Figure 3 shows how the agent with LSTM instruction encoder performs compared with our PRESS agent. There are two rare words ' mannequins ' and ' manikins ' which are not in the training dataset and confuse the LSTM agent, while, PRESS successfully maps these two 'mannequins' and 'manikins' to the correct objects.
   172

   188	(2) The second set in Figure 4 shows how the agents trained with different training strategies performs in an unseen environment. The agents trained with teacher-forcing and student-forcing both fail, while PRESS succeeds.
   189	
   190	## 4 Conclusion
   191	
   192	We present PRESS, a navigation agent based on two previously underexplored techniques in VLN: pretrained language models and stochastic action sampling. Our PRESS demonstrates robust generalization in the unseen environments, leading to a new state-of-the-art performance over many of the much more complex approaches previously proposed. As both the components of PRESS can be easily integrated, future models can consider building upon them as a strong baseline system.
   193	
   194	## Acknowledgments
   195	
   196	We thank the anonymous reviewers for their insightful comments, NSF IIS-1703166, DARPA's CwC program through ARO W911NF-15-1-0543, and the Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence.

    77	class MultiHeadedAttention(nn.Module):
    78	    """Multi-Head Attention module from "Attention is All You Need"
    79	        :cite:`DBLP:journals/corr/VaswaniSPUJGKP17`.
    80	        Similar to standard `dot` attention but uses
    81	        multiple attention distributions simulataneously
    82	        to select relevant items.
    83	        .. mermaid::
    84	           graph BT
    85	              A[key]
    86	              B[value]
    87	              C[query]
    88	              O[output]
    89	              subgraph Attn
    90	                D[Attn 1]
    91	                E[Attn 2]
    92	                F[Attn N]
    93	              end
    94	              A --> D
    95	              C --> D
    96	              A --> E
    97	              C --> E
    98	              A --> F
    99	              C --> F
   100	              D --> O
   101	              E --> O
   102	              F --> O
   103	              B --> O
   104	        Also includes several additional tricks.
   105	        Args:
   106	           head_count (int): number of parallel heads
   107	           model_dim (int): the dimension of keys/values/queries,
   108	               must be divisible by head_count

   112	    def __init__(self, head_count, model_dim, dropout=0.1,
   113	                 max_relative_positions=0):

   115	        self.dim_per_head = model_dim // head_count

   125	        self.linear_query = nn.Linear(model_dim,

   145	           value (FloatTensor): set of `key_len`

   155	        """

   265	        # aeq(batch, batch_)

   277	class PositionwiseFeedForward(nn.Module):
   278	    """ A two-layer Feed-Forward-Network with residual layer norm.
   279	    Args:
   280	        d_model (int): the size of input for the first-layer of the FFN.
   281	        d_ff (int): the hidden layer size of the second-layer
   282	            of the FNN.
   283	        dropout (float): dropout probability in :math:`[0, 1)`.
   284	    """
   285	
   286	    def __init__(self, d_model, d_ff, dropout=0.1):
   287	        super(PositionwiseFeedForward, self).__init__()
   288	        self.w_1 = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff)
   289	        self.w_2 = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model)
   290	        self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model, eps=1e-6)
   291	        self.dropout_1 = nn.Dropout(dropout)
   292	        self.relu = nn.ReLU()
   293	        self.dropout_2 = nn.Dropout(dropout)
   294	
   295	    def forward(self, x):
   296	        """Layer definition.
   297	        Args:
   298	            x: ``(batch_size, input_len, model_dim)``
   299	        Returns:
   300	            (FloatTensor): Output ``(batch_size, input_len, model_dim)``.
   301	        """
   302	
   303	        inter = self.dropout_1(self.relu(self.w_1(self.layer_norm(x))))
   304	        output = self.dropout_2(self.w_2(inter))
   305	        return output + x

   307	
   308	class TransformerEncoderLayer(nn.Module):
   309	    """
   310	    A single layer of the transformer encoder.
   311	    Args:
   312	        d_model (int): the dimension of keys/values/queries in
   313	                   MultiHeadedAttention, also the input size of
   314	                   the first-layer of the PositionwiseFeedForward.
   315	        heads (int): the number of head for MultiHeadedAttention.
   316	        d_ff (int): the second-layer of the PositionwiseFeedForward.
   317	        dropout (float): dropout probability(0-1.0).
   318	    """
   319	
   320	    def __init__(self, d_model, heads, d_ff, dropout,
   321	                 max_relative_positions=0):
   322	        super(TransformerEncoderLayer, self).__init__()
   323	
   324	        self.self_attn = MultiHeadedAttention(
   325	            heads, d_model, dropout=dropout,
   326	            max_relative_positions=max_relative_positions)
   327	        self.feed_forward = PositionwiseFeedForward(d_model, d_ff, dropout)
   328	        self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model, eps=1e-6)
   329	        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
   330	
   331	    def forward(self, inputs, mask):

   347	class TransformerEncoder(nn.Module):
   348	    """The Transformer encoder from "Attention is All You Need"
   349	    :cite:`DBLP:journals/corr/VaswaniSPUJGKP17`
   350	    .. mermaid::
   351	       graph BT
   352	          A[input]
   353	          B[multi-head self-attn]
   354	          C[feed forward]
   355	          O[output]
   356	          A --> B
   357	          B --> C
   358	          C --> O
   359	    Args:
   360	        num_layers (int): number of encoder layers
   361	        d_model (int): size of the model
   362	        heads (int): number of heads
   363	        d_ff (int): size of the inner FF layer
   364	        dropout (float): dropout parameters
   365	        embeddings (onmt.modules.Embeddings):
   366	          embeddings to use, should have positional encodings
   367	    Returns:
   368	        (torch.FloatTensor, torch.FloatTensor):
   369	        * embeddings ``(src_len, batch_size, model_dim)``
   370	        * memory_bank ``(src_len, batch_size, model_dim)``
   371	    """
   372	    lstm_num_layers = 1
   373	
   374	    def __init__(self, vocab_size, emb_hidden_size, padding_idx,
   375	                 dropout_ratio, glove, heads, d_ff, hidden_size, dec_hidden_size, top_lstm, bidirectional, max_relative_positions=0, num_layers = 1):
   376	        super(TransformerEncoder, self).__init__()
   377	
   378	        self.embedding_size = emb_hidden_size
   379	        self.num_layers = num_layers
   380	        self.embedding = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, emb_hidden_size, padding_idx)
   381	        self.use_glove = glove is not None
   382	        self.dec_hidden_size = dec_hidden_size
   383	        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
   384	        self.dropout_ratio = dropout_ratio
   385	        self.drop = nn.Dropout(p=dropout_ratio)
   386	        # if self.use_glove:
   387	        #     print('Using GloVe embedding')
   388	        #     self.embedding.weight.data[...] = torch.from_numpy(glove)
   389	        #     self.embedding.weight.requires_grad = False
   390	        #
   391	        self.top_lstm = top_lstm
   392	        self.transformer = nn.ModuleList(
   393	            [TransformerEncoderLayer(
   394	                emb_hidden_size, heads, d_ff, dropout_ratio,
   395	                max_relative_positions=max_relative_positions)
   396	             for i in range(num_layers)])
   397	        self.layer_norm = nn.LayerNorm(emb_hidden_size, eps=1e-6)
   398	
   399	        self.num_directions = 2 if bidirectional else 1
   400	        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(self.embedding_size, self.hidden_size, self.lstm_num_layers,
   401	                            batch_first=True,
   402	                            dropout=dropout_ratio if self.lstm_num_layers > 1 else 0, bidirectional=bidirectional)
   403	
   404	        self.linear_n_in = self.embedding_size if not self.top_lstm else hidden_size * self.num_directions
   405	        self.encoder2decoder_ht = nn.Linear(self.linear_n_in, dec_hidden_size)
   406	        self.encoder2decoder_ct = nn.Linear(self.linear_n_in, dec_hidden_size)
   407	
   408	    def init_state(self, batch_size):
   409	        ''' Initialize to zero cell states and hidden states.'''
   410	        h0 = Variable(torch.zeros(batch_size, self.dec_hidden_size), requires_grad=False)
   411	        c0 = Variable(torch.zeros(batch_size, self.dec_hidden_size), requires_grad=False)
   412	        return h0.to(device), c0.to(device)
   413	
   414	    def init_state_lstm(self, batch_size):
   415	        ''' Initialize to zero cell states and hidden states.'''
   416	        h0 = Variable(torch.zeros(self.num_directions, batch_size, self.hidden_size), requires_grad=False)
   417	        c0 = Variable(torch.zeros(self.num_directions, batch_size, self.hidden_size), requires_grad=False)
   418	        return h0.to(device), c0.to(device)
   419	
   420	    def forward(self, inputs, mask, lengths):
   421	
   422	        emb = self.embedding(inputs[:,:mask.shape[1]])
   423	        # Run the forward pass of every layer of the tranformer.
   424	        for layer in self.transformer:
   425	            out = layer(emb, mask.unsqueeze(1))
   426	        out = self.layer_norm(out)
   427	
   428	        if not self.top_lstm:
   429	            h0, c0 = self.init_state(inputs.size(0))
   430	            return out, h0, c0, mask
   431	
   432	        batch_size = inputs.size(0)
   433	        embeds = out
   434	        h0, c0 = self.init_state_lstm(batch_size)
   435	        packed_embeds = pack_padded_sequence(embeds, lengths, batch_first=True)
   436	        enc_h, (enc_h_t, enc_c_t) = self.lstm(packed_embeds, (h0, c0))
   437	        if self.num_directions == 2:
   438	            h_t = torch.cat((enc_h_t[-1], enc_h_t[-2]), 1)
   439	            c_t = torch.cat((enc_c_t[-1], enc_c_t[-2]), 1)
   440	        else:
   441	            h_t = enc_h_t[-1]
   442	            c_t = enc_c_t[-1] # (batch, hidden_size)

   444	        decoder_init = nn.Tanh()(self.encoder2decoder_ht(h_t))
   445	        if self.hidden_size * self.num_directions != self.dec_hidden_size:
   446	            c_t = self.encoder2decoder_ct(c_t)
   447	
   448	        ctx, lengths = pad_packed_sequence(enc_h, batch_first=True)
   449	        ctx = self.drop(ctx)
   450	        return ctx, decoder_init, c_t, mask

  1085	class SoftDotAttention(nn.Module):
  1086	    '''Soft Dot Attention. 
  1087	
  1088	    Ref: http://www.aclweb.org/anthology/D15-1166
  1089	    Adapted from PyTorch OPEN NMT.
  1090	    '''
  1091	
  1092	    def __init__(self, ctx_hidden_size, dim):
  1093	        '''Initialize layer.'''
  1094	        super(SoftDotAttention, self).__init__()
  1095	        self.linear_in = nn.Linear(dim, ctx_hidden_size, bias=False)
  1096	        self.sm = nn.Softmax(dim=1)
  1097	        self.linear_out = nn.Linear(dim + ctx_hidden_size, dim, bias=False)
  1098	        self.tanh = nn.Tanh()
  1099	
  1100	    def forward(self, h, context, mask=None, ctx_drop=None):
  1101	        '''Propagate h through the network.
  1102	
  1103	        h: batch x dim
  1104	        context: batch x seq_len x dim
  1105	        mask: batch x seq_len indices to be masked
  1106	        '''
  1107	        target = self.linear_in(h).unsqueeze(2)  # batch x dim x 1
  1108	
  1109	        # Get attention
  1110	        attn = torch.bmm(context, target).squeeze(2)  # batch x seq_len
  1111	        if mask is not None:
  1112	            # -Inf masking prior to the softmax 
  1113	            attn.data.masked_fill_(mask, -float('inf'))
  1114	        attn = self.sm(attn)
  1115	        attn3 = attn.view(attn.size(0), 1, attn.size(1))  # batch x 1 x seq_len
  1116	
  1117	        weighted_context = torch.bmm(attn3, context).squeeze(1)  # batch x dim
  1118	
  1119	        if ctx_drop is not None:
  1120	            weighted_context = ctx_drop(weighted_context)
  1121	
  1122	        h_tilde = torch.cat((weighted_context, h), 1)
  1123	        h_tilde = self.tanh(self.linear_out(h_tilde))
  1124	        return h_tilde, attn

  1127	class EltwiseProdScoring(nn.Module):
  1128	    '''
  1129	    Linearly mapping h and v to the same dimension, and do an elementwise
  1130	    multiplication and a linear scoring
  1131	    '''
  1132	
  1133	    def __init__(self, h_dim, a_dim, dot_dim=256):
  1134	        '''Initialize layer.'''
  1135	        super(EltwiseProdScoring, self).__init__()
  1136	        self.linear_in_h = nn.Linear(h_dim, dot_dim, bias=True)
  1137	        self.linear_in_a = nn.Linear(a_dim, dot_dim, bias=True)
  1138	        self.linear_out = nn.Linear(dot_dim, 1, bias=True)
  1139	
  1140	    def forward(self, h, all_u_t, mask=None):
  1141	        '''Propagate h through the network.
  1142	
  1143	        h: batch x h_dim
  1144	        all_u_t: batch x a_num x a_dim
  1145	        '''
  1146	        target = self.linear_in_h(h).unsqueeze(1)  # batch x 1 x dot_dim
  1147	        context = self.linear_in_a(all_u_t)  # batch x a_num x dot_dim
  1148	        eltprod = torch.mul(target, context)  # batch x a_num x dot_dim
  1149	        logits = self.linear_out(eltprod).squeeze(2)  # batch x a_num
  1150	        return logits

  1263	class AttnDecoderLSTM(nn.Module):
  1264	    ''' An unrolled LSTM with attention over instructions for decoding navigation actions. '''
  1265	
  1266	    def __init__(self, input_action_size, output_action_size, embedding_size, ctx_hidden_size, hidden_size,
  1267	                      dropout_ratio, feature_size, panoramic, action_space, ctrl_feature, ctrl_f_net, att_ctx_merge, ctx_dropout_ratio, params=None):
  1268	        super(AttnDecoderLSTM, self).__init__()
  1269	        self.embedding_size = embedding_size
  1270	        self.feature_size = feature_size + (128 if panoramic else 0)
  1271	        self.ctx_hidden_size = ctx_hidden_size
  1272	        self.hidden_size = hidden_size
  1273	        self.drop = nn.Dropout(p=dropout_ratio)
  1274	        self.panoramic = panoramic
  1275	        self.action_space = action_space
  1276	        self.ctrl_feature = ctrl_feature
  1277	        self.ctrl_f_net = ctrl_f_net
  1278	        self.ctx_drop = nn.Dropout(p=ctx_dropout_ratio)
  1279	
  1280	        self.dec_h_type = params['dec_h_type']
  1281	
  1282	        action_hidden_size = hidden_size
  1283	        self.att_ctx_merge = None
  1284	        if att_ctx_merge in ['mean', 'cat', 'max', 'sum']:
  1285	            if att_ctx_merge == 'cat':
  1286	                self.att_ctx_merge = torch.flatten
  1287	                action_hidden_size = 3 * hidden_size
  1288	            elif att_ctx_merge == 'mean':
  1289	                self.att_ctx_merge = torch.mean
  1290	            elif att_ctx_merge == 'max':
  1291	                self.att_ctx_merge = torch_max_not_return_idx
  1292	            elif att_ctx_merge == 'sum':
  1293	                self.att_ctx_merge = torch.sum
  1294	
  1295	        if not self.panoramic:
  1296	            LSTM_n_in = embedding_size + feature_size  # action_embedding + original single view feature
  1297	        else:
  1298	            if self.action_space == 6:
  1299	                LSTM_n_in = embedding_size + feature_size + self.feature_size  # attented multi-view feature (single feature +128)
  1300	            else:  # if self.action_space == -1:
  1301	                LSTM_n_in = self.feature_size * 2  # action feature + attented multi-view feature
  1302	                #LSTM_n_in = feature_size + self.feature_size * 2  # img feature + action feature + attented multi-view feature
  1303	
  1304	        self.lstm = nn.LSTMCell(LSTM_n_in, hidden_size)
  1305	        self.attention_layer = SoftDotAttention(ctx_hidden_size, hidden_size)
  1306	        # panoramic feature
  1307	        if self.panoramic:
  1308	            self.visual_attention_layer = VisualSoftDotAttention(hidden_size, self.feature_size)
  1309	        else:
  1310	            self.visual_attention_layer = None
  1311	
  1312	        # panoramic action space
  1313	        self.u_begin, self.embedding = None, None
  1314	        if self.action_space == 6:
  1315	            self.embedding = nn.Embedding(input_action_size, embedding_size)
  1316	            self.decoder2action = nn.Linear(action_hidden_size, output_action_size)
  1317	        else:
  1318	            self.u_begin = Variable(torch.zeros(self.feature_size), requires_grad=False).to(device)
  1319	            self.decoder2action = EltwiseProdScoring(action_hidden_size, self.feature_size)
  1320	            #self.decoder2action = EltwiseProdScoring(action_hidden_size+self.feature_size, self.feature_size) # debug
  1321	
  1322	        if self.ctrl_feature:
  1323	            if self.ctrl_f_net =='deconv':
  1324	                self.decoder2feature = DeconvFeature(action_hidden_size, feature_size)
  1325	            elif self.ctrl_f_net == 'linear':  # linear
  1326	                self.decoder2feature = LinearFeature(action_hidden_size, feature_size)
  1327	            elif self.ctrl_f_net == 'imglinear':
  1328	                self.decoder2feature = LinearFeature(action_hidden_size + feature_size, feature_size)
  1329	            elif self.ctrl_f_net == 'nonlinear':
  1330	                self.decoder2feature = NonLinearFeature(action_hidden_size, 1024, feature_size)
  1331	            elif self.ctrl_f_net == 'imgnl':
  1332	                self.decoder2feature = NonLinearFeature(action_hidden_size + feature_size, 1024, feature_size)
  1333	        else:
  1334	            self.decoder2feature = None
  1335	
  1336	    def forward(self, action_prev, u_prev, u_features,  # teacher_u_feature,
  1337	                feature, feature_all, h_0, c_0, ctx, ctx_mask=None):  #, action_prev_feature
  1338	        ''' Takes a single step in the decoder LSTM (allowing sampling).
  1339	        action: batch x 1
  1340	        feature: batch x feature_size
  1341	        h_0: batch x hidden_size
  1342	        c_0: batch x hidden_size
  1343	        ctx: batch x seq_len x dim
  1344	        ctx_mask: batch x seq_len - indices to be masked
  1345	        '''
  1346	
  1347	        if self.panoramic:  # feature.dim()==3:
  1348	            feature2, alpha_v = self.visual_attention_layer(h_0, feature_all)
  1349	
  1350	        if self.action_space == 6:
  1351	            if self.panoramic:
  1352	                feature = torch.cat((feature, feature2), 1)
  1353	            action_embeds = self.embedding(action_prev.view(-1, 1))  # (batch, 1, embedding_size)
  1354	            action_embeds = action_embeds.squeeze()
  1355	        else: # bug: todo

  1358	
  1359	        concat_input = torch.cat((action_embeds, feature2), 1)
  1360	        #concat_input = torch.cat((feature, action_embeds, feature2), 1)
  1361	        drop = self.drop(concat_input)
  1362	
  1363	        h_1, c_1 = self.lstm(drop, (h_0, c_0))
  1364	        h_1_drop = self.drop(h_1)
  1365	
  1366	        if self.att_ctx_merge is not None:
  1367	            temp, alpha = torch.zeros(h_1_drop.size(0), len(ctx), self.hidden_size).to(device), []
  1368	            for si in range(len(ctx)):

  1370	                temp[:, si, :] = h_tilde
  1371	                alpha.append(alpha_si)  # for plot
  1372	
  1373	            h_tilde = self.att_ctx_merge(temp, 1)
  1374	        else:
  1375	            h_tilde, alpha = self.attention_layer(h_1_drop, ctx, ctx_mask)
  1376	
  1377	        if self.action_space == 6:
  1378	            logit = self.decoder2action(h_tilde)  # (100, 6)
  1379	        else:
  1380	            logit = self.decoder2action(h_tilde, u_features)
  1381	            #logit = self.decoder2action(action_input, u_features)
  1382	
  1383	        pred_f = None
  1384	        if self.ctrl_feature:
  1385	            if self.ctrl_f_net == 'imglinear' or self.ctrl_f_net == 'imgnl': #
  1386	                #aux_input = torch.cat((h_1_drop, feature), 1) # with img feature h_1_drop
  1387	                aux_input = torch.cat((h_tilde, feature), 1)  # with img feature h_tilde
  1388	                pred_f = self.decoder2feature(aux_input)  # , teacher_u_feature)
  1389	            else:
  1390	                pred_f = self.decoder2feature(h_tilde) #, teacher_u_feature)
  1391	
  1392	        if self.dec_h_type == 'vc':
  1393	            return h_tilde, c_1, alpha, logit, pred_f  # h_tilde

   135	def speaker_rank(speaker, speaker_weights, speaker_merge, beam_candidates, this_completed, traversed_lists, index_count):  # todo: this_completed is not sorted!! so not corresponding to beam_candidates
   136	    cand_obs, cand_actions, multi = [], [], isinstance(beam_candidates[0]['instr_encoding'], list)

   144	        else:

   148	        for si, sub_cand_instr in enumerate(cand_instr):

   154	    else:
   155	        speaker_scored_candidates, _ = \
   156	            speaker._score_obs_actions_and_instructions(
   157	                cand_obs, cand_actions, cand_instr, feedback='teacher')
   158	        assert len(speaker_scored_candidates) == len(beam_candidates)
   159	
   160	    follower_scores = []
   161	    speaker_scores = []
   162	
   163	    score_merge = {'mean':np.mean,'max':np.max,'min':np.min}[speaker_merge]
   164	
   165	    for i, candidate in enumerate(beam_candidates):  # different to speaker follower, our beam_candidates is not nested, we already got a subset from the outside of this function, so we do not need flatten it before enumerate
   166	        speaker_scored_candidate = speaker_scored_candidates[i]

   172	            candidate['speaker_score'] = speaker_scored_candidate['score']

   184	            assert path_from_last_to_next[-1].world_state.viewpointId \

   211	        best_ix, best_cand = max(

  1096	    def train(self, encoder_optimizer, decoder_optimizer, n_iters, aux_n_iter, feedback='teacher'):
  1097	        ''' Train for a given number of iterations '''
  1098	        assert feedback in self.feedback_options
  1099	        self.feedback = feedback
  1100	        self.encoder.train()
  1101	        self.decoder.train()
  1102	        self.losses = []
  1103	        self.losses_ctrl_f = []
  1104	        epo_inc = 0
  1105	        encoder_optimizer.zero_grad()
  1106	        decoder_optimizer.zero_grad()
  1107	
  1108	        for iter in range(1, n_iters + 1):
  1109	            #encoder_optimizer.zero_grad()
  1110	            #decoder_optimizer.zero_grad()
  1111	
  1112	            self.rollout()
  1113	            if (not self.decoder.ctrl_feature) or (iter % aux_n_iter):
  1114	                self.loss.backward()
  1115	            else:
  1116	                self.loss_ctrl_f.backward()
  1117	
  1118	            if self.clip_gradient != 0: # clip gradient
  1119	                clip_gradient(encoder_optimizer, self.clip_gradient)
  1120	                clip_gradient(decoder_optimizer, self.clip_gradient)
  1121	
  1122	            if self.clip_gradient_norm > 0: # clip gradient norm
  1123	                torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(self.encoder.parameters(), self.clip_gradient_norm)
  1124	                torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(self.decoder.parameters(), self.clip_gradient_norm)
  1125	
  1126	            # accumulated gradients
  1127	            if (iter % self.acc_gradient_steps == 0) or (iter == n_iters):
  1128	                encoder_optimizer.step()
  1129	                decoder_optimizer.step()
  1130	
  1131	                # reset
  1132	                encoder_optimizer.zero_grad()
  1133	                decoder_optimizer.zero_grad()
  1134	
  1135	            epo_inc += self.env.epo_inc
  1136	        return epo_inc
